title: Quantum-classical crossover in fault-tolerant quantum dynamics simulation
abstract: While quantum computers promise to solve classically intractable problems, identifying the point at which fault-tolerant quantum computation outperforms the best classical algorithms for practical applications remains an outstanding challenge. Here we establish a concrete quantum-classical crossover for quantum many-body dynamics under realistic hardware conditions. We introduce a scalable fault-tolerant framework that combines coherent observable estimation with a space-time-efficient implementation of non-Clifford rotations, suppressing the residual logical errors that limit existing partially fault-tolerant approaches. A benchmark against state-of-the-art tensor-network and variational Monte Carlo algorithms reveals a concrete crossover for mixed-field Ising dynamics at modest system sizes. For a physical error rate of $p=10^{-3}$, fault-tolerant simulation requires approximately 2 hours and $3.7 \times 10^5$ physical qubits for a 100-site 1D system, whereas tensor network approaches would require about 100 years. For 2D models, where rapid entanglement growth limits the classical evolution time, we project quantum runtimes within minutes. A physical error rate of $p=10^{-4}$ leads to at least an order of magnitude reduction in qubit count ($3.1 \times 10^4$ physical qubits) and runtime (minutes for 1D and seconds for 2D). The reduction in quantum runtime arises from our improved rotation-state injection and co-design of quantum error correction and observable-estimation protocols, which jointly suppress logical-error accumulation and reduce sampling overhead. Our results establish a scalable route towards practical quantum advantage and identify quantitative engineering targets for future fault-tolerant architectures.

bite.hook: At a 10^-3 error rate, a 100-site quantum simulation takes 2 hours—while classical supercomputers would take 100 years.
tldr: The authors established a concrete quantum-classical crossover point for simulating quantum many-body dynamics under realistic hardware noise.
They introduced a scalable fault-tolerant framework combining coherent observable estimation with efficient non-Clifford rotations.
They demonstrated that a 100-site 1D system requires 3.7e5 physical qubits for a 2-hour quantum run, beating classical tensor networks.
bite.whyItMatters: Proving 'quantum advantage' for practical problems has been highly theoretical. This study provides a concrete, hardware-realistic blueprint showing exactly when and where quantum computers will permanently outrun classical supercomputers.
bite.eli5: Imagine a maze so complex that a normal supercomputer would take 100 years to find the exit. This paper shows that a realistic quantum computer, even with a few mistakes, could solve that same maze in just 2 hours.

keyNumbers.label: physical error rate
keyNumbers.context: the hardware noise level used to project a 2-hour quantum runtime and 3.7e5 physical qubits

keyNumbers.label: classical runtime
keyNumbers.context: the time required for state-of-the-art tensor network approaches to simulate the 100-site 1D system

keyNumbers.label: reduced qubit count
keyNumbers.context: the physical qubits needed if the hardware error rate is improved to 10^-4

glossary.shortDef: The ability of a quantum computer to operate correctly even when individual physical components fail or experience noise.
glossary.eli5Def: A design that lets a computer keep working perfectly even if some of its tiny parts make mistakes.

glossary.shortDef: A classical computational method used to efficiently simulate quantum many-body states with limited entanglement.
glossary.eli5Def: A clever trick that lets normal computers simulate quantum physics by focusing only on how nearby particles talk to each other.

glossary.shortDef: The physical behavior and time evolution of a mathematical model of ferromagnetism in statistical mechanics.
glossary.eli5Def: A classic physics model that describes how a grid of tiny atomic magnets flip and interact with each other.

glossary.shortDef: Quantum operations outside the Clifford group, necessary to achieve universal quantum computation.
glossary.eli5Def: Special, complex turns of a qubit that are incredibly hard for normal computers to copy, making them the secret sauce of quantum speed.
